questions.question: The Hasse diagrams of all the lattices with up to four elements are ________ (write all the relevant Hasse diagrams).
questions.to_find: How many non-isomorphic lattices have at most 4 elements (and draw their Hasse diagrams).

understand.plain: A lattice is a partially ordered set in which every pair of elements has a least upper bound (join) and a greatest lower bound (meet). We must list, up to isomorphism, every lattice with 1, 2, 3 or 4 elements. For each size we draw only the order shapes that satisfy the lattice rule, then count the distinct Hasse diagrams. The total comes out to 5.

keywords.explain: a poset where every two elements have a unique join (lub) and a unique meet (glb)
keywords.example: a single 4-element chain is a lattice; so is the 'diamond' 2x2 square

keywords.explain: the picture of a poset drawn with only cover edges, larger elements placed higher
keywords.example: a vertical line of dots is the Hasse diagram of a chain

keywords.explain: two lattices are counted once if one can be relabelled into the other while preserving the order
keywords.example: all 2-element chains are the same lattice no matter how the dots are named

understand.visual_alt: Five Hasse diagrams: a single point, a 2-chain, a 3-chain, a 4-chain, and the 4-element diamond (2x2 square).

given.aim: Count and draw every lattice (up to isomorphism) having 1, 2, 3 or 4 elements.

terms.meaning: a single point; trivially a lattice (it is its own top and bottom)
terms.example: the set {x} ordered by x <= x

terms.meaning: with 2 elements only the chain works; with 3 elements only the chain works (a 3-element 'V' or 'inverted V' has no top or no bottom, so it is not a lattice)
two shapes survive: the chain 0 < a < b < 1 and the diamond where two incomparable middle elements sit between a common bottom and top
terms.example: the powerset of a 2-set ordered by inclusion is the diamond

terms.meaning: every pair must have a unique join and a unique meet; a shape with two incomparable maxima (no top) fails because that pair has no upper bound
terms.example: the 4-element 'N' poset is not a lattice
terms.connects: this filter is why only 5 of the many posets count

given.plan: Enumerate posets by size, keep only those that are lattices (a top, a bottom, unique joins and meets), and add up: 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 5.

steps.title: Sizes 1, 2 and 3
steps.formula_raw: every pair {x,y} has unique lub(x,y) and glb(x,y)
steps.apply: size 1: the single point is a lattice (1). size 2: the only poset that is a lattice is the chain 0 < 1 (1). size 3: a chain 0 < m < 1 is a lattice, but the 'V' (one bottom, two incomparable tops) has no join for the two tops, and the inverted 'V' has no meet for the two bottoms, so only the chain qualifies (1).
steps.note: antichains of size >= 2 are never lattices because incomparable maxima have no common upper bound

steps.title: Size 4: keep only valid lattices
steps.formula_raw: shapes with a unique top and bottom and unique pairwise join/meet
steps.apply: the 4-element chain 0 < a < b < 1 is a lattice. The diamond (bottom 0, top 1, two incomparable middles a and b) is a lattice: a join b = 1, a meet b = 0. Other 4-element posets (the 'N', the 'V with a tail', two incomparable tops, etc.) fail the lattice test, so size 4 contributes 2 lattices.
steps.note: the diamond is exactly the powerset lattice (2^S, subset) for a 2-element set S

steps.title: Add the counts
steps.apply: sum the lattices found for sizes 1, 2, 3, 4: 1 (point) + 1 (2-chain) + 1 (3-chain) + 2 (4-chain and diamond) = 5.
steps.note: all five shapes are drawn in the figure above

solution.result: There are 5 non-isomorphic lattices with up to four elements: the 1-point lattice, the 2-, 3- and 4-element chains, and the 4-element diamond (2x2 square).

questions.formula_note: The lattice definition (unique join and meet for every pair, hence a top and a bottom) is the filter that rules out antichains and 'V'/'N' shapes, leaving exactly 5 diagrams.

questions.question: In the lattice defined by the Hasse diagram given in the following figure, how many complements does the element 'e' have? The diagram has top element a and bottom element f, with a covering b, c and g; b covering e; c covering d; and e, g, d each covering f (chains a-b-e-f, a-g-f and a-c-d-f). A. 2   B. 3   C. 0   D. 1
questions.to_find: The number of complements of the element e in the given lattice.

understand.plain: In a bounded lattice with top a (the 1) and bottom f (the 0), a complement of an element x is any element y with x join y = a and x meet y = f. So to count the complements of e, we test every other element y: compute the join (lub) and the meet (glb) of e and y from the diagram, and keep those y that hit the top on the join and the bottom on the meet. Three elements qualify, so e has 3 complements.

keywords.explain: a lattice with a greatest element 1 (here a) and a least element 0 (here f)
keywords.example: in this diagram a is above everything and f is below everything

keywords.explain: an element y such that x join y = 1 (top) and x meet y = 0 (bottom)
keywords.example: y is a complement of e when e join y = a and e meet y = f

keywords.term: join and meet from a Hasse diagram
keywords.explain: the join is the lowest common element reachable going up; the meet is the highest common element reachable going down
keywords.example: e join d: the smallest element above both e and d is the top a

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 420' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Hasse diagram for the 1997 complement problem'><title>Lattice with top a and bottom f</title><desc>Top a covers b, c and g. b covers e, c covers d, and e, g, d each cover the bottom f. The complements of e are c, d and g (highlighted).</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='40' x2='90' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='40' x2='270' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='40' x2='180' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='130' x2='90' y2='280' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='130' x2='270' y2='280' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='150' x2='180' y2='380' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='280' x2='180' y2='380' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='280' x2='180' y2='380' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle class='bg' cx='180' cy='40' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='176' y='45'>a</text><circle class='bg' cx='90' cy='130' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='86' y='135'>b</text><circle class='hi' cx='270' cy='130' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='266' y='135'>c</text><circle class='hi' cx='180' cy='160' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='176' y='165'>g</text><circle class='ok' cx='90' cy='280' r='13'/><text class='lbl' fill='#FFFFFF' x='84' y='285'>e</text><circle class='hi' cx='270' cy='280' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='266' y='285'>d</text><circle class='bg' cx='180' cy='380' r='13'/><text class='lbl ink' x='176' y='385'>f</text><text class='sub mut' x='30' y='415'>green = e ; purple = complements c, d, g</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A six-level Hasse diagram where the complements of e are the elements c, d and g.

given.aim: Find every element y with e join y = a (top) and e meet y = f (bottom).

terms.meaning: the greatest element of the lattice; a join with anything is at most a
terms.example: e join b = a only if going up from both lands at a
terms.connects: the join condition for a complement must equal a

terms.meaning: the least element of the lattice; a meet with anything is at least f
terms.example: e meet g = f because the only element below both is f
terms.connects: the meet condition for a complement must equal f

terms.meaning: the element under test; it sits on the chain a-b-e-f (so e is below b and a, above f)
terms.example: elements <= e are {e, f}; elements >= e are {e, b, a}
terms.connects: its up-set and down-set decide the joins and meets with every other element

terms.meaning: all elements other than e: b, c, d, g (and the bounds a, f)
terms.example: b shares the element b above e (e join b = b, not a), so b fails
terms.connects: each candidate is checked against both conditions

given.plan: For each element y != e, read the join e join y and meet e meet y off the diagram; y is a complement exactly when the join is a and the meet is f. Count the survivors.

steps.title: Set up the complement test
steps.formula_raw: y is a complement of e  <=>  e join y = a  and  e meet y = f
steps.apply: the lattice is bounded with top a and bottom f. e lies on the chain a > b > e > f, so the elements >= e are {e, b, a} and the elements <= e are {e, f}. We will join and meet e with each of b, c, d, g.
steps.note: only b shares an upper element (b) strictly below a with e, which makes its join too small

steps.title: Test b and the three other elements
steps.formula_raw: e join b = b != a (fails); e join c = a, e meet c = f; e join d = a, e meet d = f; e join g = a, e meet g = f
steps.apply: e join b = b (since e <= b), so b is not a complement. For c: the smallest element above both e and c is a, and the largest below both is f, so e join c = a and e meet c = f -> c is a complement. The same holds for d (a-c-d-f chain) and for g (a-g-f chain): each joins with e at a and meets with e at f.
steps.note: c, d, g all sit on chains disjoint from e except at the top a and bottom f, which is exactly what makes them complements

steps.title: Count the complements
steps.formula_raw: complements of e = {c, d, g}  =>  count = 3
steps.apply: exactly three elements satisfy both conditions, namely c, d and g. Hence e has 3 complements.

solution.result: The element e has 3 complements (c, d and g), so the answer is B. 3.

questions.formula_note: The complement rule e join y = 1 and e meet y = 0 is the key: reading joins/meets off the Hasse diagram shows c, d and g each meet both conditions, giving 3 complements.

questions.question: Let S = {a, b} and let 2^S (the powerset of S) be the set of all subsets of S. Consider the binary relation 'subset (set inclusion)' on 2^S. Draw the Hasse diagram corresponding to the lattice (2^S, subset).
questions.answer: N/A (descriptive). The powerset 2^S = {empty set, {a}, {b}, {a,b}} ordered by inclusion is a 4-element lattice whose Hasse diagram is the diamond (2x2 square): the empty set at the bottom, {a,b} at the top, and the two singletons {a} and {b} as incomparable middle elements; each singleton covers the empty set and is covered by {a,b}.
questions.to_find: Draw the Hasse diagram of the powerset lattice (2^S, subset) for S = {a, b}.

understand.plain: The powerset of S = {a,b} has 2^2 = 4 subsets: the empty set, {a}, {b}, and {a,b}. Ordering them by subset inclusion gives a lattice in which the join of two subsets is their union and the meet is their intersection. The picture is the classic 'diamond': empty set at the bottom, the full set on top, and the two singletons side by side in the middle with no inclusion between them.

keywords.explain: the set of all subsets of S; for |S| = n it has 2^n elements
X <= Y means X is a subset of Y; this partial order makes 2^S a poset
in (2^S, subset) the lub of X and Y is X union Y and the glb is X intersection Y

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 360' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Hasse diagram of the powerset lattice of a two-element set'><title>Powerset lattice (2^S, subset) for S = {a,b}</title><desc>The empty set at the bottom, the singletons {a} and {b} in the middle (incomparable), and {a,b} at the top, forming a diamond.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='60' x2='80' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='60' x2='280' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='180' x2='180' y2='300' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='180' x2='180' y2='300' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle class='hi' cx='180' cy='60' r='16'/><text class='sub ink' x='190' y='45'>{a,b}</text><circle class='bg' cx='80' cy='180' r='16'/><text class='sub ink' x='30' y='185'>{a}</text><circle class='bg' cx='280' cy='180' r='16'/><text class='sub ink' x='300' y='185'>{b}</text><circle class='hi' cx='180' cy='300' r='16'/><text class='sub ink' x='150' y='340'>empty set</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A diamond Hasse diagram: empty set at the bottom, {a,b} at the top, with {a} and {b} as the two incomparable middle nodes.

given.aim: Build the poset (2^S, subset) for S = {a,b} and draw its Hasse diagram, verifying it is a lattice.

terms.meaning: the ground set with two elements
terms.example: its subsets are listed by choosing whether each of a, b is in or out

terms.connects: these are the four nodes of the Hasse diagram

terms.meaning: X is covered by Y when Y = X union {one extra element}
terms.example: empty set is covered by {a} and by {b}; each singleton is covered by {a,b}
terms.connects: these covers are the four edges of the diagram

terms.meaning: the empty set is the least element (0) and {a,b} is the greatest element (1)
terms.example: empty set subset every subset; every subset subset {a,b}
terms.connects: their presence makes the poset a bounded lattice

given.plan: List the four subsets, find the cover relations from single-element additions, place the empty set at the bottom and {a,b} at the top with the singletons in the middle, and confirm union/intersection give unique joins/meets.

steps.title: List the powerset and the order
steps.formula_raw: 2^S = {empty set, {a}, {b}, {a,b}}, ordered by X subset Y
steps.apply: with S = {a,b} the four subsets are empty set, {a}, {b}, {a,b}. Under inclusion: empty set subset {a} subset {a,b}, empty set subset {b} subset {a,b}, while {a} and {b} are incomparable (neither contains the other).

steps.title: Find covers and draw the diagram
steps.apply: each cover adds exactly one element. Put the empty set at the bottom, the two singletons {a} and {b} side by side in the middle, and {a,b} at the top, drawing the four cover edges. This is the diamond (2x2 square).
steps.note: the two singletons are drawn at the same height because they are incomparable

steps.title: Confirm it is a lattice
steps.formula_raw: X join Y = X union Y,  X meet Y = X intersection Y
steps.apply: join is union and meet is intersection, both always subsets of S, so every pair has a unique lub and glb. For instance {a} join {b} = {a,b} and {a} meet {b} = empty set. Hence (2^S, subset) is a (Boolean) lattice.
steps.note: this 4-element diamond is exactly one of the five small lattices from question 4.6.4

solution.result: The Hasse diagram is the diamond: empty set at the bottom, {a,b} at the top, and the incomparable singletons {a} and {b} in the middle; join is union and meet is intersection, so (2^S, subset) is a lattice.

questions.formula_note: The powerset-lattice idea (join = union, meet = intersection) shows (2^{a,b}, subset) is the 4-element diamond; it is descriptive (key marks it N/A).

questions.question: The following is the Hasse diagram of the poset [{a, b, c, d, e}, <=]: the top element a covers the three incomparable elements b, c and d, and each of b, c, d covers the bottom element e (a 'diamond' with three middle atoms). The poset is: A. not a lattice   B. a lattice but not a distributive lattice   C. a distributive lattice but not a Boolean algebra   D. a Boolean algebra
questions.answer: B. a lattice but not a distributive lattice
questions.to_find: Classify the poset: lattice / distributive / Boolean, given the M3 'diamond' Hasse diagram.

understand.plain: The diagram has a single top a, a single bottom e, and three incomparable middle elements b, c, d, each linked only to a and e. This shape is the well-known lattice M3. Every pair has a join (a if they differ, else the larger) and a meet (e if they differ), so it is a lattice. But M3 fails the distributive law, which immediately rules out 'distributive' and 'Boolean'. So the correct classification is 'a lattice but not a distributive lattice'.

keywords.explain: a lattice where x meet (y join z) = (x meet y) join (x meet z) for all x, y, z
keywords.example: every powerset lattice (subsets under inclusion) is distributive

keywords.explain: the 5-element lattice with one top, one bottom, and three incomparable atoms in between; the classic non-distributive lattice
keywords.example: b meet (c join d) = b meet a = b, but (b meet c) join (b meet d) = e join e = e, so b != e shows non-distributivity

keywords.explain: a complemented distributive lattice; it must first be distributive
keywords.example: the diamond fails distributivity, so it cannot be Boolean

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 320' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='M3 diamond Hasse diagram for the 2005 poset'><title>The M3 lattice on {a,b,c,d,e}</title><desc>Top a covers three incomparable atoms b, c, d; each atom covers the bottom e. This is a lattice but not distributive.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='190' y1='50' x2='80' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='190' y1='50' x2='190' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='190' y1='50' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='160' x2='190' y2='270' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='190' y1='160' x2='190' y2='270' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='300' y1='160' x2='190' y2='270' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle class='hi' cx='190' cy='50' r='14'/><text class='lbl ink' x='185' y='55'>a</text><circle class='bg' cx='80' cy='160' r='14'/><text class='lbl ink' x='75' y='165'>b</text><circle class='bg' cx='190' cy='160' r='14'/><text class='lbl ink' x='185' y='165'>c</text><circle class='bg' cx='300' cy='160' r='14'/><text class='lbl ink' x='295' y='165'>d</text><circle class='hi' cx='190' cy='270' r='14'/><text class='lbl ink' x='185' y='275'>e</text><text class='sub mut' x='40' y='305'>M3: lattice, not distributive</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The M3 diamond: top a, bottom e, and three incomparable middle atoms b, c, d, the classic non-distributive lattice.

given.aim: Decide whether the M3 poset is a lattice, whether it is distributive, and whether it is Boolean.

terms.meaning: a is the greatest element (1) and e is the least element (0)
terms.connects: guarantees joins and meets exist at the extremes

terms.meaning: three pairwise incomparable elements each covering only e and covered only by a
terms.example: b and c are incomparable; b join c = a, b meet c = e
terms.connects: their pairwise joins/meets are unique, so the structure is a lattice

terms.meaning: x meet (y join z) = (x meet y) join (x meet z) for all triples
terms.example: tested with x=b, y=c, z=d below
terms.connects: the property that separates a plain lattice from a distributive one

terms.meaning: a Boolean algebra must be both distributive and complemented
terms.example: since distributivity already fails, Boolean is impossible
terms.connects: lets us discard options C and D at once

given.plan: Confirm every pair has a unique join and meet (so it is a lattice), then test the distributive law on b, c, d; the failure rules out distributive and Boolean, leaving option B.

steps.title: Show it is a lattice
steps.formula_raw: for incomparable x, y: x join y = a, x meet y = e; otherwise the larger/smaller
steps.apply: any two distinct atoms (e.g. b, c) have join a and meet e; an atom with a gives join a and meet that atom; an atom with e gives join that atom and meet e. Every pair has a unique lub and glb, and a top a and bottom e exist, so the poset is a lattice. Option A is wrong.
steps.note: this is the standard 5-element lattice M3

steps.title: Test the distributive law
steps.formula_raw: b meet (c join d) vs (b meet c) join (b meet d)
steps.apply: compute the left side: c join d = a, so b meet (c join d) = b meet a = b. Compute the right side: b meet c = e and b meet d = e, so (b meet c) join (b meet d) = e join e = e. Since b != e, the distributive law fails, so the lattice is not distributive. Option C is wrong.
steps.note: M3 is the prototypical non-distributive lattice (any non-distributive lattice contains M3 or N5)

steps.title: Rule out Boolean and conclude
steps.apply: a Boolean algebra must be distributive, but we just showed this lattice is not distributive, so it cannot be Boolean. Option D is wrong. The only consistent description is 'a lattice but not a distributive lattice'.
steps.note: additionally, in a Boolean algebra complements are unique, whereas here b has three complements c, d and... reinforcing the failure

solution.result: The poset is a lattice (M3) but fails the distributive law, hence is not distributive and not Boolean: the answer is B. a lattice but not a distributive lattice.

questions.formula_note: The distributive test x meet (y join z) = (x meet y) join (x meet z) fails on the M3 diamond (b vs e), so it is a lattice but neither distributive nor Boolean.

formulas.name: Lattice (Join and Meet)
formulas.plain: a poset is a lattice if every pair of elements x, y has a unique least upper bound x join y (the join) and a unique greatest lower bound x meet y (the meet); a finite lattice always has a top 1 and a bottom 0
formulas.whenToUse: to test whether a Hasse diagram is a lattice and to compute joins/meets of elements

terms.means: least upper bound of x and y, read upward in the Hasse diagram
greatest lower bound of x and y, read downward in the Hasse diagram

formulas.trap: a poset with two incomparable maximal elements is not a lattice because that pair has no join; do not assume any finite poset is automatically a lattice
formulas.reference: Discrete mathematics; partially ordered sets and lattices

formulas.name: Complement in a Bounded Lattice
formulas.latex: y \text{ is a complement of } x \iff x \vee y = 1 \;\wedge\; x \wedge y = 0
formulas.plain: in a bounded lattice with top 1 and bottom 0, an element y is a complement of x when their join is the top and their meet is the bottom; an element may have zero, one, or several complements
formulas.whenToUse: to count or find complements of an element in a bounded lattice given by a Hasse diagram

terms.means: the greatest (top) element of the lattice
the least (bottom) element of the lattice
x is the element under test; y ranges over candidate complements

formulas.trap: complements need NOT be unique outside a Boolean (distributive complemented) lattice; check both conditions x join y = 1 AND x meet y = 0 for every candidate

formulas.name: Powerset Lattice (2^S, subset)
formulas.latex: (\,2^{S},\subseteq\,):\quad X \vee Y = X \cup Y,\quad X \wedge Y = X \cap Y
formulas.plain: the set of all subsets of S ordered by inclusion forms a lattice in which the join of two subsets is their union and the meet is their intersection; it is a Boolean lattice with 2^|S| elements, bottom empty set and top S
formulas.whenToUse: to draw or analyse the lattice of subsets of a finite set under set inclusion

terms.means: the powerset of S, the collection of all subsets of S
union of subsets X and Y, equal to their join
intersection of subsets X and Y, equal to their meet

formulas.trap: incomparable subsets (like {a} and {b}) sit at the same level; do not draw an edge between them since neither contains the other

formulas.name: Distributive Lattice Law
formulas.plain: a lattice is distributive when meet distributes over join (equivalently join over meet) for all elements; the diamond M3 and the pentagon N5 are the two smallest lattices that violate this, and any non-distributive lattice contains one of them
formulas.whenToUse: to decide whether a lattice is distributive by testing the law on a likely-failing triple

terms.means: arbitrary lattice elements; pick incomparable ones to expose a failure
join and meet of the lattice

formulas.trap: one failing triple is enough to prove non-distributivity; in M3 take x, y, z to be the three middle atoms and the law breaks immediately
formulas.reference: Discrete mathematics; distributive lattices (M3 / N5 theorem)

formulas.name: Boolean Algebra (Complemented Distributive Lattice)
formulas.plain: a Boolean algebra is a bounded lattice that is both distributive and complemented (every element has a complement, which is then unique); failing distributivity or complementation disqualifies it
formulas.whenToUse: to classify whether a lattice is a Boolean algebra after checking distributivity and complements

terms.means: the bottom and top of the bounded lattice
the element with x join x' = 1 and x meet x' = 0, unique in a Boolean algebra

formulas.trap: Boolean requires distributivity FIRST; a non-distributive lattice (like M3) can never be Boolean even if every element happens to have some complement
